verses.text: 'Fear not: for I have redeemed thee... thou art mine. When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee.' God owns and keeps His people. Through flood and fire His presence goes with them, so they need not fear.

'Fear not: for I have redeemed thee... thou art mine. When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee.' God owns and keeps His people. Through flood and fire His presence goes with them, so they need not fear.

'Fear not: for I have redeemed thee... thou art mine. When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee.' God owns and keeps His people. Through flood and fire His presence goes with them, so they need not fear.

Because they are precious and honoured in His sight, He will gather their offspring from every direction. His love values them highly. God scatters and regathers His own according to His tender purpose.

He brings forth the blind and deaf people as His witnesses, 'that ye may know and believe me,' for before Him no god was formed. They testify to the one true God. The people of God are called to witness to His sole deity.

'I, even I, am the LORD; and beside me there is no saviour'; none can deliver out of His hand. He alone saves. There is no rescue and no rival apart from the living God.

For their sake He sends judgment on Babylon and recalls how He made 'a way in the sea' to drown Pharaoh's army. Past redemption foretells future. The God of the exodus will redeem His people again.

'Remember ye not the former things... behold, I will do a new thing,' making rivers in the desert for His chosen. Fresh mercies are promised. God's grace is ever new, providing for the people He formed for His praise.

Yet they have not truly called on Him but 'made me to serve with thy sins,' wearying Him with iniquities. They burdened God with their guilt. Empty religion that clings to sin grieves the God it claims to serve.

'I, even I, am he that blotteth out thy transgressions for mine own sake, and will not remember thy sins.' Pardon is pure grace. God forgives freely for His own name's sake, not for any worth in us.
